cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is about what the International Olympic Committee (IOC) was concerned about regarding the Beijing 2008 Olympic medals that have jade inlay. The options are A through D. First, I need to recall the information from the passage. The passage mentions that the IOC was concerned about the risk of fragility when the jade ring design was proposed. They required that the jade rings be strong enough to withstand a 2-meter drop test, and the Shanghai Mint even raised it to 3 meters. The result is that the medals can survive a 3-meter drop with only minor cracks on the jade rings. Looking at the options: A) the medals will be worth less – The passage doesn't mention anything about the monetary value or worth of the medals, so this seems unrelated. B) the medals are too difficult to make – While the passage does mention the complexity of bonding jade and metal, the IOC's specific concern was about fragility, not the manufacturing difficulty. C) competitors don't like the design – There's no mention of competitors' opinions in the text. The concern was technical, not aesthetic. D) jade is fragile and easy to break up – This aligns with the IOC's concern about fragility. The requirement for a drop test
